mnemonics: Think of granularity like a library: you can lock a single book (fine), a whole shelf (coarse), or the entire building (very coarse). Multiple Granularity Locking (MGL) is the system that lets you efficiently manage all these different-sized locks at once.
keyPoints: Granularity refers to the size of the data item being locked (e.g., a table, page, or row).
Multiple Granularity Locking (MGL) allows a transaction to lock data items at different levels of the database hierarchy.
The protocol uses intention locks (IS, IX) to signal that a transaction intends to lock a descendant (smaller item) of a currently locked node.
commonMistakes: Assuming that locking a table (coarse granularity) is always better for performance; it can severely limit concurrency.
Forgetting to acquire the appropriate intention lock (e.g., IS or IX) on a parent node (e.g., a table) before locking a child node (e.g., a row).
